## Security & Compliance

> âš ď¸Ź LOCKED â€” These rules cannot be overridden.

### Secrets & Credentials

#### No hardcoded secrets
NEVER hardcode API keys, passwords, tokens, private keys, or connection strings in code/config/comments. BLOCK any PR with literal secrets. MUST reference secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Doppler) or environment variables. Use `.env.example` (no values), never commit `.env`.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### Injection Prevention

#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER build SQL/NoSQL/LDAP queries with string concatenation or template literals containing user input. MUST use parameterized queries (`$1`, `?`, `:param`), prepared statements, or ORM bindings. Example: `db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId])` instead of string concat.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### Data Privacy & PII

#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER set `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify`, or `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. Use proper certificate chains; for local dev, use mkcert for locally-trusted certs. Self-signed certs in application code = CRITICAL security failure.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
MUST use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication and AES-256+ for storage. Hash passwords with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2â€”NEVER MD5/SHA. NEVER implement custom crypto; use established libraries (libsodium, crypto). No plaintext passwords, unencrypted DB connections, or HTTP for sensitive APIs.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### Authorization & Access Control

#### Authorization on every endpoint
ALWAYS verify both authentication (who) and authorization (what they can do) server-side. Check object ownership on `/:id` routes. NEVER rely on client-side guards alone. Prevent IDOR by validating the caller owns the resource.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

#### Never implement custom authentication
NEVER build custom password hashing, JWT validation, session management, or OAuth flows. ALWAYS use the project's auth system or a proven provider (Auth0, Firebase, Cognito, Clerk). Custom auth creates timing attacks and token vulnerabilities.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### Destructive Operations

#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, or `rm -rf` without explicit confirmation flag. MUST use soft deletes, implement rollback scripts, and require human approval for irreversible operations in production. AI tools must never autonomously execute destructive commands.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

#### Database changes through migrations only
MUST route all schema changes through the migration tool (Drizzle/Prisma/Alembic/Flyway). NEVER execute direct ALTER TABLE or manual SQL against production. Every migration MUST include a rollback script.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### Error Exposure

#### No internal details in error responses
NEVER expose stack traces, database errors, SQL, file paths, or internal IDs in API responses. ALWAYS return: `{"error":{"code":"SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE","message":"User-safe text","requestId":"uuid"}}`. Route all exceptions through centralized error handler before sending to client.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### PII in logs
NEVER log, expose, or return PII (names, emails, SSNs, tokens, passwords, IPs, phone numbers). Mask sensitive fields before logging: use hash, redaction, or tokenization. Example: log `user_id: <hash>` instead of `email: user@example.com`. Unmasked PII in logs creates compliance breaches and security vulnerabilities.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

## Architecture & Patterns

### Error Handling

#### No silent error swallowing
NEVER use empty catch blocks or bare `except: pass`. Every caught error MUST be logged with context, re-thrown, or handled with retry/fallback. Example: `catch(e) { logger.error('Payment retry failed', e); throw e; }`. Silent failures hide data corruption and cause undebuggable outages.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### Testing

#### Tests required for new logic
MUST write tests for all new functions, endpoints, or behavioral changes: happy path, one error case, and boundary conditions. Follow the project's test framework. Place tests in the established test directory alongside source files.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### repository pattern
MUST use the repository layer for all data access. Route handlers call repos, repos call data stores. Do not query directly from handlers or services.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

## Conventions

### structured logging
ALWAYS log structured objects with fields (timestamp, level, context, error), never plain strings. Example: `{"level":"error","action":"auth_fail","userId":"...","message":"..."}`. Enables parsing, filtering, and monitoring.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### error response format
All error responses MUST follow the project's established format consistently. Identify the format in existing error handlers and replicate it exactly. Do NOT invent new error structures or field names.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->

### Tailwind CSS usage
MUST use Tailwind utility classes only; NEVER write custom CSS. In shared components, use @apply sparingly for reusable patterns (e.g., button base styles). Define component-specific utilities in tailwind.config.js, not inline.
<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
